---
title: "Classification Analysis: Airline Passenger Satisfaction"
author: "DSC532 Group Project"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output:
  html_document:
    toc: true
    toc_float: true
    toc_depth: 3
    number_sections: true
    theme: cosmo
    code_folding: show
    fig_width: 10
    fig_height: 6
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo = TRUE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE,
  fig.align = "center", fig.width = 10, fig.height = 6
)
```

# Setup and Libraries

```{r libraries}
# Install packages if needed (uncomment on first run)
# install.packages(c("tidyverse", "janitor", "skimr", "corrplot", "caret",
#                     "MASS", "class", "e1071", "glmnet", "pROC",
#                     "knitr", "kableExtra", "gridExtra", "boot"))

library(tidyverse)
library(janitor)
library(skimr)
library(corrplot)
library(caret)
library(MASS)
library(class)
library(e1071)
library(glmnet)
library(pROC)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)
library(gridExtra)
library(boot)

set.seed(42)

# --- Custom theme for report-quality plots ---
theme_report <- function(base_size = 13) {
  theme_minimal(base_size = base_size) %+replace%
    theme(
      plot.title       = element_text(face = "bold", size = base_size + 2,
                                      hjust = 0,
                                      margin = ggplot2::margin(t = 0, r = 0, b = 8, l = 0)),
      plot.subtitle    = element_text(color = "grey40", size = base_size - 1,
                                      margin = ggplot2::margin(t = 0, r = 0, b = 10, l = 0)),
      axis.title       = element_text(face = "bold", size = base_size),
      axis.text        = element_text(size = base_size - 2, color = "grey30"),
      strip.text       = element_text(face = "bold", size = base_size - 1),
      legend.title     = element_text(face = "bold", size = base_size - 1),
      legend.text      = element_text(size = base_size - 2),
      legend.position  = "bottom",
      panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
      plot.margin      = ggplot2::margin(10, 10, 10, 10)
    )
}

# Consistent colour palette
palette_sat <- c("neutral or dissatisfied" = "#E74C3C",
                 "satisfied"               = "#3498DB")
palette_2   <- c("#3498DB", "#E74C3C")
```

# Data Loading and Overview

The dataset contains airline passenger satisfaction survey responses. We load the pre-split training and test sets separately to preserve the integrity of the evaluation pipeline — the test set will not be touched until the final model evaluation.

```{r load-data}
train_raw <- read_csv("train.csv")
test_raw  <- read_csv("test.csv")

cat("Training set dimensions:", dim(train_raw), "\n")
cat("Test set dimensions:", dim(test_raw), "\n")
```

```{r first-look}
glimpse(train_raw)
```

```{r head-data}
head(train_raw)
```

```{r summary-data}
summary(train_raw)
```

The training set has `r nrow(train_raw)` observations and `r ncol(train_raw)` variables. The first two columns (`...1` and `id`) are administrative identifiers with no predictive value. The target variable `satisfaction` is binary: "satisfied" or "neutral or dissatisfied".

# Data Cleaning

## Removing Identifiers and Standardising Column Names

We remove the unnamed index column (`...1`) and the `id` column since they carry no predictive information, and apply `clean_names()` to ensure consistent naming throughout the analysis.

```{r clean-names}
train_clean <- train_raw %>%
  clean_names() %>%
  dplyr::select(-any_of(c("x1", "x_1")), -id)

test_clean <- test_raw %>%
  clean_names() %>%
  dplyr::select(-any_of(c("x1", "x_1")), -id)

colnames(train_clean)
```

After cleaning we have `r ncol(train_clean)` variables: 4 continuous (age, flight distance, departure delay, arrival delay), 14 ordinal service ratings (1–5 scale), and 4 categorical variables (gender, customer type, type of travel, class), plus the target.

## Missing Values Analysis

```{r missing-values}
bind_rows(
  train_clean %>%
    summarise(across(everything(), ~ sum(is.na(.)))) %>%
    pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "variable", values_to = "na_count") %>%
    mutate(dataset = "train", na_percent = round(na_count / nrow(train_clean) * 100, 3)),

  test_clean %>%
    summarise(across(everything(), ~ sum(is.na(.)))) %>%
    pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "variable", values_to = "na_count") %>%
    mutate(dataset = "test", na_percent = round(na_count / nrow(test_clean) * 100, 3))
) %>%
  filter(na_count > 0) %>%
  dplyr::select(dataset, variable, na_count, na_percent) %>%
  arrange(desc(na_count)) %>%
  kable(col.names = c("Dataset", "Variable", "Missing Count", "Missing %")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = "striped", full_width = FALSE)
```

Missing values are present exclusively in `arrival_delay_in_minutes` — approximately 0.3% of both the training and test sets. Simply deleting the affected rows would discard potentially informative observations. We therefore pursue a principled imputation strategy. Before deciding on the imputation model, we first conduct a thorough EDA to understand the structure of the data and, in particular, to identify which variables are most strongly associated with `arrival_delay_in_minutes` — this evidence will guide our choice of imputation predictors.

# Exploratory Data Analysis

Before fitting any model, we conduct a thorough exploratory analysis. Understanding the structure of the data is essential for making informed modelling decisions. Our EDA serves three purposes: (1) examining the marginal distributions of predictors and their relationship with the response, (2) identifying potential data quality issues such as outliers, and (3) building the empirical justification for the imputation strategy we will apply to the missing `arrival_delay_in_minutes` values.

## Target Variable Distribution

```{r target-dist}
train_clean %>%
  count(satisfaction) %>%
  mutate(prop = round(n / sum(n) * 100, 1)) %>%
  kable(col.names = c("Class", "Count", "Percentage (%)")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = "striped", full_width = FALSE)
```

```{r target-barplot, fig.height=5, fig.width=7}
train_clean %>%
  count(satisfaction) %>%
  mutate(prop = n / sum(n),
         label = paste0(round(prop * 100, 1), "%")) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(satisfaction, -n), y = n, fill = satisfaction)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.6, show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_text(aes(label = label), vjust = -0.5, size = 5, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = palette_sat) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::comma, expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.1))) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Passenger Satisfaction",
       subtitle = "Training set (n = 103,904)",
       x = "Satisfaction Class", y = "Count") +
  theme_report()
```

The response variable exhibits a moderate class imbalance: approximately 56.7% of passengers report being neutral or dissatisfied, while 43.3% report being satisfied. This degree of imbalance is not severe enough to warrant resampling techniques, but it does mean that raw classification accuracy can be misleading — a naive classifier predicting the majority class for every observation would achieve 56.7% accuracy without learning any structure from the predictors. We therefore adopt AUC as our primary model comparison metric, supplemented by F1 score, precision, and recall, to ensure that our evaluation captures a model's ability to discriminate between the two classes rather than merely reflecting the base rate.

## Distributions of Continuous Variables

```{r cont-dist-overall, fig.height=6, fig.width=10}
train_clean %>%
  dplyr::select(age, flight_distance,
                departure_delay_in_minutes, arrival_delay_in_minutes) %>%
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "variable", values_to = "value") %>%
  mutate(variable = dplyr::recode(variable,
    "age" = "Age (years)",
    "flight_distance" = "Flight Distance (miles)",
    "departure_delay_in_minutes" = "Departure Delay (min)",
    "arrival_delay_in_minutes" = "Arrival Delay (min)")) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = value)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 40, fill = "#3498DB", color = "white", alpha = 0.85) +
  facet_wrap(~ variable, scales = "free", ncol = 2) +
  labs(title = "Marginal Distributions of Continuous Predictors",
       subtitle = "Each panel uses an independent scale to highlight distributional shape",
       x = NULL, y = "Frequency") +
  theme_report() +
  theme(strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 12))
```

**Age** is moderately spread across adults, with a slight bimodal pattern suggesting two main passenger age groups.

**Flight distance** is right-skewed, with the majority of flights being short-to-medium haul and a long upper tail likely for intercontinental routes.

**Departure and arrival delay** are both heavily right-skewed with a mass of near-zero values and a sparse but long upper tail. These will be examined in detail in the outlier analysis below.

```{r cont-dist-by-sat, fig.height=6, fig.width=10}
train_clean %>%
  dplyr::select(satisfaction, age, flight_distance,
                departure_delay_in_minutes, arrival_delay_in_minutes) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = -satisfaction, names_to = "variable", values_to = "value") %>%
  mutate(variable = dplyr::recode(variable,
    "age" = "Age (years)",
    "flight_distance" = "Flight Distance (miles)",
    "departure_delay_in_minutes" = "Departure Delay (min)",
    "arrival_delay_in_minutes" = "Arrival Delay (min)")) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = value, fill = satisfaction)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 40, color = "white", alpha = 0.55, position = "identity") +
  facet_wrap(~ variable, scales = "free", ncol = 2) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = palette_sat, name = "Satisfaction") +
  labs(title = "Continuous Predictor Distributions by Satisfaction Class",
       subtitle = "Overlaid histograms reveal where class-conditional distributions diverge",
       x = NULL, y = "Frequency") +
  theme_report() +
  theme(strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 12))
```

Breaking the distributions down by satisfaction class reveals two notable patterns. First, satisfied passengers tend to be somewhat older, with the satisfied distribution shifted towards middle age. Second, satisfied passengers are more heavily concentrated among longer flights, suggesting that business travellers (who tend to fly further) drive satisfaction. Departure and arrival delays do not show a strong visual separation by class, which is consistent with the finding that most passengers experience little to no delay.

### Delay Distribution Analysis

```{r delay-zoom, fig.height=5, fig.width=12}
p_dep <- train_clean %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = departure_delay_in_minutes, fill = satisfaction)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 5, alpha = 0.55, position = "identity", color = "white") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 200)) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = palette_sat, name = "Satisfaction") +
  labs(title = "Departure Delay (Zoomed: 0\u2013200 min)",
       x = "Departure Delay (minutes)", y = "Frequency") +
  theme_report(base_size = 12)

p_arr <- train_clean %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = arrival_delay_in_minutes, fill = satisfaction)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 5, alpha = 0.55, position = "identity", color = "white") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 200)) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = palette_sat, name = "Satisfaction") +
  labs(title = "Arrival Delay (Zoomed: 0\u2013200 min)",
       x = "Arrival Delay (minutes)", y = "Frequency") +
  theme_report(base_size = 12)

grid.arrange(p_dep, p_arr, ncol = 2)
```

Both delay variables have extreme right tails. We investigate whether the very high values are genuine extreme events or data entry errors.

### Delay Outlier Investigation

```{r delay-tail-analysis}
train_clean %>%
  dplyr::select(departure_delay_in_minutes, arrival_delay_in_minutes) %>%
  summarise(across(everything(), list(
    min    = ~ min(., na.rm = TRUE),
    p25    = ~ quantile(., 0.25, na.rm = TRUE),
    median = ~ median(., na.rm = TRUE),
    p75    = ~ quantile(., 0.75, na.rm = TRUE),
    p95    = ~ quantile(., 0.95, na.rm = TRUE),
    p99    = ~ quantile(., 0.99, na.rm = TRUE),
    max    = ~ max(., na.rm = TRUE)
  ))) %>%
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = c("variable", "stat"),
               names_sep = "_(?=[^_]+$)") %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = stat, values_from = value) %>%
  kable(digits = 1) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = "striped", full_width = FALSE)
```

```{r extreme-counts}
train_clean %>%
  summarise(
    dep_over_180  = sum(departure_delay_in_minutes > 180, na.rm = TRUE),
    dep_over_300  = sum(departure_delay_in_minutes > 300, na.rm = TRUE),
    dep_over_500  = sum(departure_delay_in_minutes > 500, na.rm = TRUE),
    arr_over_180  = sum(arrival_delay_in_minutes > 180, na.rm = TRUE),
    arr_over_300  = sum(arrival_delay_in_minutes > 300, na.rm = TRUE),
    arr_over_500  = sum(arrival_delay_in_minutes > 500, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) %>%
  kable() %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = "striped", full_width = FALSE)
```

```{r extreme-rows}
train_clean %>%
  filter(departure_delay_in_minutes > 500) %>%
  dplyr::select(departure_delay_in_minutes, arrival_delay_in_minutes,
                flight_distance, class, type_of_travel,
                departure_arrival_time_convenient) %>%
  arrange(desc(departure_delay_in_minutes))
```

Inspecting the extreme cases confirms that very long departure delays are always accompanied by comparably long arrival delays — the two variables move in lockstep. Delays exceeding 500 minutes (over 8 hours) are operationally plausible and can occur in practice due to severe weather, mechanical failures, or flight rescheduling to the following day. We therefore retain these observations, as removing them would introduce selection bias and prevent our models from generalising to such rare but real scenarios.

## Flight Distance Outlier Analysis

```{r flight-dist-quantiles}
train_clean %>%
  dplyr::select(flight_distance) %>%
  summarise(across(everything(), list(
    min    = ~ min(., na.rm = TRUE),
    p25    = ~ quantile(., 0.25, na.rm = TRUE),
    median = ~ median(., na.rm = TRUE),
    p75    = ~ quantile(., 0.75, na.rm = TRUE),
    p95    = ~ quantile(., 0.95, na.rm = TRUE),
    p99    = ~ quantile(., 0.99, na.rm = TRUE),
    max    = ~ max(., na.rm = TRUE)
  ))) %>%
  pivot_longer(everything(),
               names_to = c("variable", "stat"),
               names_sep = "_(?=[^_]+$)") %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = stat, values_from = value) %>%
  kable(digits = 1) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = "striped", full_width = FALSE)
```

While the upper tail of `flight_distance` reaches 4,983 miles, this is entirely consistent with long-haul intercontinental routes. No implausible values were identified and no observations are removed.

## Service Rating Distributions

The 14 service attributes are measured on a discrete 1–5 scale (with 0 occasionally representing "not applicable").

```{r rating-dist, fig.height=10, fig.width=12}
rating_vars <- c("inflight_wifi_service", "departure_arrival_time_convenient",
                  "ease_of_online_booking", "gate_location", "food_and_drink",
                  "online_boarding", "seat_comfort", "inflight_entertainment",
                  "on_board_service", "leg_room_service", "baggage_handling",
                  "checkin_service", "inflight_service", "cleanliness")

train_clean %>%
  dplyr::select(satisfaction, all_of(rating_vars)) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = -satisfaction, names_to = "variable", values_to = "value") %>%
  count(variable, value, satisfaction) %>%                         # pre-aggregate here
  mutate(variable = gsub("_", " ", variable) %>% tools::toTitleCase()) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = factor(value), y = n, fill = satisfaction)) +
  geom_col(position = "stack", width = 0.7) +                     # geom_col on counts
  facet_wrap(~ variable, scales = "free_y", ncol = 4) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = palette_sat, name = "Satisfaction") +
  labs(title = "Service Rating Distributions by Satisfaction Class",
       subtitle = "Each bar is stacked by class; strong predictors show visibly different compositions at higher ratings",
       x = "Rating (0\u20135 scale)", y = "Frequency") +
  theme_report(base_size = 11) +
  theme(strip.text = element_text(size = 10, face = "bold"))
```

Most service attributes concentrate in the 3--4 range, reflecting a broadly moderate to positive perception of airline services. The stacked bars reveal which service dimensions have the strongest association with the response: variables such as `inflight_entertainment`, `seat_comfort`, and `online_boarding` show a visibly different class composition at the higher rating values — passengers rating these services as 4 or 5 are predominantly in the satisfied class, whereas lower ratings are dominated by the dissatisfied class. These variables are therefore expected to carry substantial discriminative power in the classification models. In contrast, variables such as `gate_location` and `departure_arrival_time_convenient` display only minor compositional differences across rating levels, suggesting they are weaker predictors and may be candidates for removal during feature selection.

## Satisfaction by Key Categorical Variables

```{r satisfaction-by-cat, fig.height=8, fig.width=10}
make_cat_plot <- function(data, var, title) {
  data %>%
    count(!!sym(var), satisfaction) %>%
    group_by(!!sym(var)) %>%
    mutate(prop = n / sum(n)) %>%
    ggplot(aes(x = !!sym(var), y = prop, fill = satisfaction)) +
    geom_col(position = "dodge", width = 0.65) +
    scale_fill_manual(values = palette_sat, name = "Satisfaction") +
    scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 1)) +
    labs(title = title, y = "Proportion", x = NULL) +
    theme_report(base_size = 11) +
    theme(legend.position = "none")
}

p1 <- make_cat_plot(train_clean, "class", "By Travel Class")
p2 <- make_cat_plot(train_clean, "type_of_travel", "By Travel Purpose")
p3 <- make_cat_plot(train_clean, "customer_type", "By Customer Type")
p4 <- make_cat_plot(train_clean, "gender", "By Gender")

grid.arrange(p1, p2, p3, p4, ncol = 2,
             top = grid::textGrob("Satisfaction Rate by Categorical Predictors",
                                  gp = grid::gpar(fontsize = 15, fontface = "bold")),
             bottom = grid::textGrob("Blue = Satisfied; Red = Neutral or Dissatisfied",
                                     gp = grid::gpar(fontsize = 10, col = "grey40")))
```

The categorical predictors reveal substantial heterogeneity in satisfaction rates across their levels. Business class passengers are the most satisfied group by a wide margin, while Economy and Economy Plus passengers are predominantly dissatisfied — suggesting that cabin class is among the strongest predictors in the dataset. Similarly, passengers travelling for business purposes report much higher satisfaction than those on personal trips, consistent with the hypothesis that service expectations and experience differ across travel contexts. Loyal customers display higher satisfaction than disloyal customers, which may partly reflect confounding with business travel and premium cabin access rather than a pure loyalty effect. Gender, by contrast, shows virtually no difference in satisfaction rates, indicating it carries minimal discriminative power once other predictors are accounted for. These exploratory patterns motivate retaining `class`, `type_of_travel`, and `customer_type` as important categorical predictors while anticipating that `gender` may be dropped by formal variable selection procedures.

## Correlation Analysis

### Correlation Heatmap

```{r corr-numeric}
train_clean_num <- train_clean %>%
  mutate(satisfaction_num = ifelse(satisfaction == "satisfied", 1, 0)) %>%
  dplyr::select(where(is.numeric))

cor_mat <- cor(train_clean_num, use = "pairwise.complete.obs")
round(cor_mat, 2)
```

```{r corr-heatmap-numeric, fig.height=10, fig.width=11}
corrplot::corrplot(cor_mat,
         method = "color",
         order = "hclust",
         type = "upper",
         tl.cex = 0.8,
         tl.col = "grey20",
         tl.srt = 45,
         cl.cex = 0.8,
         title = "Pearson Correlation Matrix of Numeric Variables",
         mar = c(0, 0, 2, 0),
         col = colorRampPalette(c("#E74C3C", "white", "#3498DB"))(200))
```

A few key observations from the correlation matrix:

-   **Departure delay and arrival delay** are almost perfectly correlated (r ≈ 0.97), confirming that the two variables are nearly interchangeable from a linear standpoint. This is the foundation for our imputation strategy.
-   **`satisfaction_num`** (the binary-encoded target, 1 = satisfied, 0 = dissatisfied) shows moderate positive correlations with several service rating variables — particularly `online_boarding`, `inflight_entertainment`, and `seat_comfort` — and a moderate positive correlation with `flight_distance`. It is essentially uncorrelated with the delay variables, suggesting that delays alone are not the primary driver of dissatisfaction in this dataset.
-   The service rating variables form a loose cluster of moderate positive inter-correlations (r ≈ 0.2–0.5), which is expected: passengers who rate one service dimension highly tend to rate others highly too. However, these correlations are not high enough to cause serious multicollinearity concerns.

Note: interpreting the correlation of binary variables (like `satisfaction_num`) with continuous predictors should be done with caution, as Pearson correlation assumes linearity and does not capture threshold or interaction effects.

## Arrival Delay: Justifying the Imputation Strategy

Before imputing the \~0.3% of missing `arrival_delay_in_minutes` values, we systematically examine its relationship with every other variable. The goal is to determine the minimal sufficient predictor set for the imputation model.

### Relationship with Numeric Variables

```{r arrival-corr-bar, fig.height=6}
arr_cors <- cor_mat["arrival_delay_in_minutes", ]
arr_cors_df <- data.frame(
  variable = names(arr_cors),
  correlation = arr_cors
) %>%
  filter(variable != "arrival_delay_in_minutes") %>%
  arrange(abs(correlation))

ggplot(arr_cors_df, aes(x = reorder(variable, abs(correlation)),
                        y = correlation, fill = abs(correlation) > 0.1)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.7) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("FALSE" = "#95A5A6", "TRUE" = "#E74C3C"),
                    labels = c("|r| < 0.1 (negligible)", "|r| > 0.1 (meaningful)")) +
  labs(title = "Correlation of Arrival Delay with All Other Numeric Variables",
       subtitle = "Only departure delay shows a practically meaningful linear association",
       x = NULL, y = "Pearson Correlation (r)", fill = NULL) +
  theme_report() +
  theme(legend.position = "top")
```

The bar chart makes the pattern unmistakably clear: `departure_delay_in_minutes` has a near-perfect correlation with `arrival_delay_in_minutes` (r ≈ 0.97), while every other numeric variable — including age, flight distance, and all 14 service ratings — has an absolute correlation effectively equal to zero (\|r\| \< 0.05). This means that once we account for departure delay, no additional numeric variable provides meaningful predictive power for the missing arrival delay values.

### Relationship with Categorical Variables

```{r arrival-delay-boxplots-zoomed, fig.height=6, fig.width=10}
train_clean %>%
  dplyr::select(arrival_delay_in_minutes, satisfaction, gender,
                customer_type, type_of_travel, class) %>%
  pivot_longer(-arrival_delay_in_minutes,
               names_to = "variable", values_to = "category") %>%
  mutate(variable = recode(variable,
    "satisfaction" = "Satisfaction",
    "gender" = "Gender",
    "customer_type" = "Customer Type",
    "type_of_travel" = "Travel Purpose",
    "class" = "Cabin Class")) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = category, y = arrival_delay_in_minutes, fill = category)) +
  geom_boxplot(outlier.alpha = 0.05, outlier.size = 0.5, width = 0.6) +
  facet_wrap(~ variable, scales = "free_x") +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0, 180)) +
  labs(title = "Arrival Delay by Categorical Variables",
       subtitle = "Zoomed to 0\u2013180 minutes; medians and IQRs are nearly identical across all groups",
       y = "Arrival Delay (minutes)", x = NULL) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  theme_report() +
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 15, hjust = 1))
```

### Imputation Justification Summary

Combining the numerical and categorical evidence:

1.  **Departure delay** is the only variable with a meaningful relationship with arrival delay (Pearson r ≈ 0.97).
2.  All other **numeric variables** have near-zero correlations (\|r\| \< 0.05).
3.  **Categorical variables** show no practically meaningful differences in arrival delay across their levels.

Therefore, a simple linear regression `arrival_delay ~ departure_delay` is the most appropriate and parsimonious imputation model. Adding further predictors would contribute negligible information while increasing model complexity unnecessarily.

# Missing Value Imputation

## Imputation Model: Simple Linear Regression

Based on the EDA evidence, we fit a simple linear regression of arrival delay on departure delay using all complete cases in the **training set only**. Fitting on the training set prevents data leakage — the imputation model sees no information from the test set.

```{r imputation-model}
train_reg <- train_clean %>%
  dplyr::select(departure_delay_in_minutes, arrival_delay_in_minutes) %>%
  na.omit()

# Fit the imputation model on complete training cases
delay_model <- lm(arrival_delay_in_minutes ~ departure_delay_in_minutes,
                  data = train_reg)
summary(delay_model)
```

The model summary reveals an extremely strong fit: the R² is approximately 0.93, meaning that departure delay alone explains 93% of the variance in arrival delay. The F-statistic is enormous and the p-value for the coefficient is effectively zero, confirming that the linear relationship is both highly significant and practically strong. The regression coefficient (\~1.02) indicates that each additional minute of departure delay translates into approximately one additional minute of arrival delay on average — an intuitive and operationally sensible result.

```{r imputation-scatter, fig.height=5, fig.width=7}
ggplot(train_reg, aes(x = departure_delay_in_minutes, y = arrival_delay_in_minutes)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.15, color = "#3498DB", size = 0.4) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "#E74C3C", se = TRUE, linewidth = 1.2) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 200), ylim = c(0, 200)) +
  labs(title = "Imputation Model: Arrival Delay vs. Departure Delay",
       subtitle = paste0("Fitted OLS line ; Pearson r = ",
                         round(cor(train_reg$departure_delay_in_minutes,
                                   train_reg$arrival_delay_in_minutes), 4)),
       x = "Departure Delay (minutes)", y = "Arrival Delay (minutes)") +
  theme_report()
```

## Cross-Validation of Imputation Accuracy

To get an honest estimate of how well the imputation model generalises (rather than relying on in-sample R²), we perform 5-fold cross-validation and report the mean squared error.

```{r imputation-cv}
delay_model_glm <- glm(arrival_delay_in_minutes ~ departure_delay_in_minutes,
                       data = train_reg)
set.seed(123)
cv_result <- cv.glm(train_reg, delay_model_glm, K = 10)

cat("10-fold CV Mean Squared Error (imputation model):", round(cv_result$delta[1], 4), "\n")
cat("Root MSE:", round(sqrt(cv_result$delta[1]), 2), "minutes\n")
```

The cross-validated RMSE gives us a realistic estimate of the prediction error when imputing a missing arrival delay value from the corresponding departure delay. Given that departure delays span from 0 to over 1,500 minutes, an RMSE on the order of a few minutes confirms that the imputation model is highly accurate and will introduce only negligible noise into the imputed values.

## Applying the Imputation

```{r keep-raw-versions}
# Preserve the versions with missing values for reference
train_with_na <- train_clean
test_with_na  <- test_clean
```

```{r apply-imputation}
# Impute using the model fitted on training data only (no leakage)
train_clean$arrival_delay_in_minutes[is.na(train_clean$arrival_delay_in_minutes)] <-
  predict(delay_model,
          newdata = train_clean[is.na(train_clean$arrival_delay_in_minutes), ])

cat("Remaining NAs in train:", sum(is.na(train_clean$arrival_delay_in_minutes)), "\n")
```

After imputation the training set is complete. We impute only the training data here for exploratory analysis; the test set (`test_with_na`) is left untouched and will be preprocessed within the model evaluation pipeline to avoid any data leakage.

# Classification Modelling

We now proceed to classification. Our response variable is `satisfaction` (binary: "satisfied" vs "neutral or dissatisfied"). R modelling functions such as `glm()`, `lda()`, and `qda()` automatically handle categorical predictors by creating the appropriate dummy variables internally. For `class` (3 levels), R creates 2 dummies; for binary categoricals like `gender`, it creates 1 dummy.

```{r prepare-train}
train <- train_clean
train$satisfaction <- factor(train$satisfaction)
```

## Feature Scaling

Before fitting any classification model we scale the predictor variables. This serves two purposes:

1.  **Coefficient comparability** — with all numeric predictors on similar scales, the magnitude of each logistic regression coefficient directly reflects its contribution to the log-odds of satisfaction, making it easy to rank and compare predictors regardless of their original measurement units.
2.  **Numerical stability** — the IRLS solver in `glm()` converges more reliably when variables do not span vastly different ranges (e.g., `flight_distance` in the thousands versus service ratings on a 0–5 scale).

We apply two scaling strategies that respect the nature of each variable group:

-   **Standard scaling (z-score)** for the four *continuous* variables (`age`, `flight_distance`, `departure_delay_in_minutes`, `arrival_delay_in_minutes`). Each variable is centred at its training-set mean and divided by its training-set standard deviation. A one-unit change in a standardised coefficient corresponds to a one-SD shift in the original variable — a natural unit for comparing variables with different spreads.
-   **Min-Max scaling** for the 14 *ordinal rating* variables (discrete 0–5 scale). Each rating is rescaled to $[0,\,1]$ via $(x - \min) / (\max - \min)$. This preserves the equal spacing between adjacent integer levels while placing all ratings on a common range. A coefficient of, say, 2.0 for a min-max scaled rating means that moving from the worst possible rating (0) to the best (5) changes the log-odds of satisfaction by 2.0.

All scaling parameters — means, standard deviations, minima, and maxima — are **computed from the training set only**. The test set is not scaled here; it will be preprocessed within the model evaluation pipeline.

```{r feature-scaling}
cont_vars <- c("age", "flight_distance",
               "departure_delay_in_minutes", "arrival_delay_in_minutes")

rating_vars <- c("inflight_wifi_service", "departure_arrival_time_convenient",
                 "ease_of_online_booking", "gate_location", "food_and_drink",
                 "online_boarding", "seat_comfort", "inflight_entertainment",
                 "on_board_service", "leg_room_service", "baggage_handling",
                 "checkin_service", "inflight_service", "cleanliness")

# --- Standard Scaling for Continuous Variables (parameters from train only) ---
cont_means <- colMeans(train[, cont_vars])
cont_sds   <- apply(train[, cont_vars], 2, sd)

train_scaled <- train

train_scaled[, cont_vars] <- scale(train[, cont_vars],
                                   center = cont_means, scale = cont_sds)

# --- Min-Max Scaling for Rating Variables (parameters from train only) ---
rating_mins   <- apply(train[, rating_vars], 2, min)
rating_ranges <- apply(train[, rating_vars], 2, max) - rating_mins

for (v in rating_vars) {
  train_scaled[[v]] <- (train[[v]] - rating_mins[v]) / rating_ranges[v]
}

# Verification
cat("Continuous variables — means (should be ~0) and SDs (should be ~1):\n")
round(rbind(mean = colMeans(train_scaled[, cont_vars]),
            sd   = apply(train_scaled[, cont_vars], 2, sd)), 4)
```

```{r scaling-verify-ratings}
cat("Rating variables — observed range after min-max scaling (should be [0, 1]):\n")
apply(train_scaled[, rating_vars], 2, range) %>%
  t() %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  setNames(c("min", "max")) %>%
  kable(digits = 4) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = "striped", full_width = FALSE)
```

## Logistic Regression on Full Training Set

We begin our classification analysis with logistic regression — the natural starting point for a binary response. We fit the model using `glm()` with `family = binomial` on all available predictors. The model estimates the conditional probability $P(Y = \text{satisfied} \mid X)$ via the logistic function, and the coefficients describe how each predictor shifts the log-odds of satisfaction. We fit on the **entire training set** to obtain the most stable coefficient estimates and $p$-values for interpretation.

### Model Fitting

```{r logistic-full-fit}
glm_full <- glm(satisfaction ~ ., data = train_scaled, family = binomial)
summary(glm_full)
```

### Coefficient Interpretation

```{r logistic-coef-table}
coef_table <- summary(glm_full)$coefficients %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  rownames_to_column("Variable") %>%
  rename(Estimate = Estimate, SE = `Std. Error`,
         z_value = `z value`, p_value = `Pr(>|z|)`) %>%
  mutate(
    Odds_Ratio = round(exp(Estimate), 4),
    Significant_0.05 = ifelse(p_value < 0.05, "Yes", "No"),
    p_value = signif(p_value, 4)
  ) %>%
  arrange(p_value)

coef_table %>%
  kable(digits = 4) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = "striped", full_width = FALSE) %>%
  scroll_box(height = "400px")
```

In logistic regression, the coefficients are on the log-odds scale: a positive coefficient means that increasing that predictor raises the log-odds (and therefore the probability) of the positive class ("satisfied"), while a negative coefficient decreases it. The **odds ratio** (`exp(Estimate)`) gives a more intuitive interpretation: an odds ratio greater than 1 means the predictor increases the odds of satisfaction, less than 1 decreases them.

Because predictors are now scaled, coefficients are directly comparable in magnitude across all variables: **continuous variable** coefficients represent the log-odds change per one standard deviation; **rating variable** coefficients represent the change when moving from the minimum to the maximum of the full rating scale (0 → 5); **categorical variable** coefficients are unchanged, representing the difference from the reference category holding all others fixed.

Key observations from the coefficient table:

-   `Customer_type` is likely the most influential predictor: being a Loyal Customer increases the odds of satisfaction by a factor of 8.54, keeping all other variables constant. -`Type_of_travel` has a strong negative effect: passengers traveling for Personal Travel have substantially lower odds of satisfaction (OR = 0.067) compared to business travelers. -`Class` also plays a significant role: compared to the reference category (Business class), passengers in Economy (OR = 0.45) and Economy Plus (OR = 0.37) are significantly less likely to be satisfied.

-   The **Null deviance** vs **Residual deviance** comparison in the summary output shows a substantial reduction, confirming that the predictors collectively explain a meaningful portion of the variation in satisfaction.

-   Most predictors are statistically significant at the 5% level. The main exception is flight_distance (p = 0.1287), which does not appear to contribute meaningfully after controlling for the other variables. \## Feature Selection: Forward Stepwise via `step()`

The full model includes all 23 predictors, but the coefficient table reveals that several have large $p$-values, suggesting they add little discriminative power after accounting for the remaining variables. As discussed in ISLR (Section 6.1), selecting a parsimonious subset of predictors can reduce variance without substantially increasing bias, particularly when some features are nearly redundant. Since the dataset has 23 dummy-encoded predictors (exceeding the 15-variable limit of `bestglm`) and \$\sim\$100k observations, we use R's `step()` function with forward selection to identify the best model under both AIC and BIC.

The full model includes all 23 predictors. Although most coefficients are statistically significant, using all variables may still introduce problems related to model complexity, redundancy, and multicollinearity. In particular, several service-rating variables measure closely related aspects of the passenger experience, so they may contain overlapping information that is not fully obvious from the correlation matrix alone. To obtain a more parsimonious model, we therefore apply forward stepwise feature selection using step(), and compare models selected under both AIC and BIC. Selecting a parsimonious subset of predictors can reduce variance without substantially increasing bias.

**How `step()` works:** Starting from the null (intercept-only) model, forward selection evaluates every possible single-variable addition at each iteration and picks the one that most improves the information criterion. The process stops when no addition improves the criterion.

**Information criteria recap:**

-   **AIC** = $-2\ell + 2k$: moderate penalty per parameter; tends to retain more variables.
-   **BIC** = $-2\ell + k\log n$: stronger penalty (for our training size of \~100k, $\log n \approx 11.5$); favours more parsimonious models.

### Forward Selection with AIC

```{r step-forward-aic, cache=TRUE}
full_mod <- glm(satisfaction ~ ., data = train_scaled, family = binomial)
null_mod <- glm(satisfaction ~ 1, data = train_scaled, family = binomial)

# Forward selection using AIC (default k = 2)
step_fwd_aic <- stats::step(null_mod,
                     scope = list(lower = null_mod, upper = full_mod),
                     direction = "forward",
                     trace = 0)

cat("=== Forward Selection — AIC ===\n")
cat("Predictors selected:", length(coef(step_fwd_aic)) - 1, "\n")
cat("AIC:", round(AIC(step_fwd_aic), 2), "\n")
cat("BIC:", round(BIC(step_fwd_aic), 2), "\n\n")
summary(step_fwd_aic)
```

### Forward Selection with BIC

```{r step-forward-bic, cache=TRUE}
# Forward selection using BIC (k = log(n))
step_fwd_bic <- stats::step(null_mod,
                     scope = list(lower = null_mod, upper = full_mod),
                     direction = "forward",
                     k = log(nrow(train_scaled)),
                     trace = 1)

cat("=== Forward Selection — BIC ===\n")
cat("Predictors selected:", length(coef(step_fwd_bic)) - 1, "\n")
cat("AIC:", round(AIC(step_fwd_bic), 2), "\n")
cat("BIC:", round(BIC(step_fwd_bic), 2), "\n\n")
summary(step_fwd_bic)
```

```{r}
# Likelihood Ratio Test: BIC-reduced vs Full logistic regression
anova(step_fwd_bic, glm_full, test = "Chisq")
# Side-by-side comparison
data.frame(
  Model       = c("BIC Reduced (19 predictors)", "Full (23 predictors)"),
  Df          = c(df.residual(step_fwd_bic), df.residual(glm_full)),
  Deviance    = c(deviance(step_fwd_bic),    deviance(glm_full)),
  AIC         = c(AIC(step_fwd_bic),         AIC(glm_full)),
  BIC         = c(BIC(step_fwd_bic),         BIC(glm_full))
)
```

The BIC-selected model retains 19 predictors, dropping `gate_location`, `food_and_drink`, `genderMale`, and `flight_distance`. Comparing with the full model, the coefficient estimates for the retained variables change only marginally, confirming that the dropped variables were neither confounders nor suppressors. The key coefficients in the reduced model remain consistent with our earlier interpretation: `customer_typeLoyal Customer` carries a large positive effect (high odds of satisfaction), `type_of_travelPersonal Travel` a large negative effect, and the service ratings `online_boarding`, `inflight_entertainment`, and `seat_comfort` retain their strong positive associations with satisfaction. The BIC model achieves comparable deviance to the AIC model with four fewer parameters, confirming it as the more parsimonious choice without sacrificing explanatory power.

### Comparison of Selected Models

```{r step-comparison}
aic_vars <- names(coef(step_fwd_aic))[-1]
bic_vars <- names(coef(step_fwd_bic))[-1]

cat("=== Forward AIC model:", length(aic_vars), "predictors ===\n")
cat(paste(aic_vars, collapse = ", "), "\n\n")

cat("=== Forward BIC model:", length(bic_vars), "predictors ===\n")
cat(paste(bic_vars, collapse = ", "), "\n\n")

# Variables in AIC but not BIC
dropped <- setdiff(aic_vars, bic_vars)
if (length(dropped) > 0) {
  cat("Dropped by BIC (extra penalty):", paste(dropped, collapse = ", "), "\n")
} else {
  cat("Both criteria selected the same variables.\n")
}
```

The AIC forward selection retained all 23 predictors — the penalty was not strong enough to exclude any variable given the large sample size. The BIC model, with its heavier penalty ($\log n \approx 11.5$ vs 2 for AIC), dropped four predictors: `gate_location`, `food_and_drink`, `genderMale`, and `flight_distance`. These are variables contribute negligible discriminative power after accounting for the other predictors. We adopt the **BIC-selected 19-predictor model** as our reduced model going forward, as it achieves nearly the same fit with fewer parameters.

## Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA)

As an alternative to logistic regression, we now consider LDA (ISLR, Section 4.4). While logistic regression models the posterior probability $P(Y = k \mid X)$ directly, LDA takes a generative approach: it models the class-conditional density $f_k(x) = P(X \mid Y = k)$ for each class $k$ and then applies Bayes' theorem to obtain the posterior. Under the assumption that both classes share a common covariance matrix $\Sigma$ and that the class-conditional densities are multivariate Gaussian, the log-ratio of posteriors simplifies to a linear function of the predictors, yielding a linear decision boundary — hence the name. For a binary problem ($K = 2$), LDA produces a single discriminant axis (LD1) that maximises Fisher's criterion: the ratio of between-class variance to within-class variance.

Formally, LDA classifies an observation $\mathbf{x}$ to the class $k$ that maximises the discriminant score:

$$\delta_k(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{x}^\top \Sigma^{-1} \boldsymbol{\mu}_k - \tfrac{1}{2} \boldsymbol{\mu}_k^\top \Sigma^{-1} \boldsymbol{\mu}_k + \log \pi_k$$

where $\boldsymbol{\mu}_k$ is the class mean vector, $\Sigma$ is the pooled within-class covariance, and $\pi_k$ is the prior probability for class $k$.

### LDA on Full Model

```{r lda-full}
lda_full <- lda(satisfaction ~ ., data = train_scaled)
lda_full
```

### LD1 Scaling (Discriminant Coefficients)

The LD1 scaling vector defines the linear combination of predictors that forms the discriminant axis. Each coefficient indicates how much each predictor contributes to the discriminant axis — because predictors have been standardised, the magnitudes are directly comparable, with larger absolute values signalling stronger contribution to class separation.

```{r lda-full-coefficients, fig.height=7}
# Extract and sort LD1 coefficients
lda_coefs <- data.frame(
  Variable = rownames(lda_full$scaling),
  LD1      = round(lda_full$scaling[, 1], 4)
) %>%
  arrange(desc(abs(LD1)))

# Table of all coefficients
lda_coefs %>%
  kable(row.names = FALSE, col.names = c("Variable", "LD1 Coefficient"),
        caption = "LD1 Scaling Coefficients (sorted by |coefficient|)") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE)
```

```{r lda-coef-barplot, fig.height=8, fig.width=9}
lda_coefs %>%
  mutate(Variable = factor(Variable, levels = rev(Variable)),
         Direction = ifelse(LD1 > 0, "Positive (toward satisfied)",
                                      "Negative (toward dissatisfied)")) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = LD1, y = Variable, fill = Direction)) +
  geom_col(alpha = 0.85, show.legend = TRUE) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Positive (toward satisfied)" = "#3498DB",
                                "Negative (toward dissatisfied)" = "#E74C3C"),
                    name = "Direction on LD1") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linewidth = 0.6, color = "grey30") +
  labs(title = "LDA Discriminant Coefficients (LD1 Scaling Vector)",
       subtitle = "Magnitude reflects each predictor's contribution to the optimal separating direction",
       x = "LD1 Coefficient", y = NULL) +
  theme_report(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")
```

**Interpretation of the scaling vector.** The LD1 scaling vector defines the linear combination of predictors that forms the discriminant axis — the direction in feature space that best separates the two classes. The three strongest drivers of satisfaction are online_boarding (1.613), inflight_wifi_service (1.365), and customer_type: Loyal Customer (1.252), while type_of_travel: Personal Travel (-1.544) and ease_of_online_booking (-0.819) are the strongest drivers of dissatisfaction. Predictors such as genderMale (0.029) and flight_distance (0.005) contribute negligibly to the discriminant axis.

**Connection with BIC feature selection.** Reassuringly, the variables dropped by BIC in the logistic regression step — `gate_location`, `food_and_drink`, `genderMale`, and `flight_distance` — are precisely those that receive the smallest LD1 coefficients in absolute value. For instance, `genderMale` (0.029) and `flight_distance` (0.005) are the two weakest contributors to the discriminant axis, while `gate_location` and `food_and_drink` also rank near the bottom. This convergence across two independent methods (penalised likelihood selection and Fisher's discriminant) strengthens our confidence that these variables are genuinely uninformative for predicting satisfaction once the remaining predictors are accounted for.

**Group means.** We observe that predictors with larger differences in group means tend to receive larger LD1 coefficients in absolute value. For example, online_boarding (group means: 0.531 vs 0.805, difference: 0.274) has the largest positive coefficient (1.613), and type_of_travel: Personal Travel (0.492 vs 0.073, difference: 0.419) has the largest negative coefficient (-1.544). Conversely, predictors with nearly identical group means across classes receive near-zero coefficients, such as genderMale (0.487 vs 0.500, difference: 0.013, coefficient: 0.029) and departure_delay_in_minutes (0.044 vs -0.058, difference: 0.102, coefficient: 0.083). This is consistent with the LDA closed-form solution where between-class mean differences drive the discriminant axis.

### LD1 Score Distribution

Since we have a binary classification problem, LDA produces a single discriminant axis (LD1). The LD1 scores represent each observation's position along the direction that maximally separates the two classes. Visualising these scores as a histogram with kernel density estimation (KDE) overlays provides a direct view of how well LDA discriminates between satisfied and dissatisfied passengers.

```{r lda-full-ld1-histogram, fig.height=6}
lda_full_pred <- predict(lda_full)

ld1_df <- data.frame(
  LD1   = lda_full_pred$x[, 1],
  Class = train_scaled$satisfaction
)

# Compute the LDA decision threshold (midpoint of group means on LD1)
#group_means_ld1 <- tapply(ld1_df$LD1, ld1_df$Class, mean)
#lda_threshold <- mean(group_means_ld1)

ggplot(ld1_df, aes(x = LD1, fill = Class)) +
  geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)), bins = 80,
                 alpha = 0.4, position = "identity", color = "white", linewidth = 0.1) +
  geom_density(aes(color = Class), linewidth = 1.1, fill = NA) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = palette_sat) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("neutral or dissatisfied" = "#C0392B",
                                 "satisfied" = "#2980B9")) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of LD1 Discriminant Scores by Class",
       subtitle = "Histogram with kernel density overlay; overlap near zero indicates the region of highest classification uncertainty",
       x = "LD1 Score", y = "Density",
       fill = "Class", color = "Class") +
  theme_report() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

The two classes are well separated along LD1, with satisfied passengers concentrating around positive scores and dissatisfied passengers around negative scores. The overlap region near zero represents observations where the two classes are hardest to distinguish, and corresponds to the highest classification uncertainty under the model. The skewness visible in the satisfied class suggests the normality assumption may be imperfect.

## Naive Bayes on Full Training Data

Before proceeding to dimensionality reduction, we fit a Naive Bayes classifier on the full training set. The defining assumption of Naive Bayes is conditional independence: within each class, the predictors are assumed to be independent of one another. This is clearly violated in our data (e.g., departure and arrival delay are almost perfectly correlated, and service ratings exhibit moderate positive inter-correlations). Despite this, Naive Bayes often performs reasonably well in practice because the posterior probabilities only need to rank observations correctly, not be perfectly calibrated. We fit it here primarily for exploratory interpretation: the class-conditional means and standard deviations provide a concise snapshot of how each feature's distribution differs between satisfied and dissatisfied passengers.

```{r naive-bayes-full-exploration}
library(e1071)

nb_explore <- naiveBayes(satisfaction ~ ., data = train_scaled)
nb_explore
```

**Interpretation of the Naive Bayes summary.** The output shows the class-conditional means and standard deviations for each numeric predictor, as well as the conditional class probabilities for each level of the categorical predictors. Key observations:

-   The **a-priori probabilities** reflect the class balance we observed earlier (\~56.7% dissatisfied, \~43.3% satisfied).
-   Among the continuous variables, `online_boarding` and `inflight_entertainment` show the largest separation in class-conditional means — satisfied passengers rate these much higher on average — which aligns with the LDA and logistic regression findings.
-   Variables like `departure_delay_in_minutes` and `arrival_delay_in_minutes` have very similar means across both classes, confirming that delays contribute little to the satisfaction prediction.
-   For categorical predictors, `type_of_travel` and `customer_type` show large differences in conditional probabilities between classes, again consistent with the strong effects identified by logistic regression and LDA.

The Naive Bayes assumption of conditional independence between predictors is clearly violated (e.g., departure and arrival delay are almost perfectly correlated), so we do not expect Naive Bayes to be the top-performing classifier. However, its simplicity and interpretability make it a useful baseline for comparison.

## Principal Component Analysis (PCA)

Following the dimensionality reduction approach described in ISLR (Section 6.3), we apply PCA to the numeric predictor matrix before fitting PCA-based classifiers. PCA identifies orthogonal directions (principal components) of maximum variance in the data, effectively rotating the coordinate system so that the first few components capture most of the variability. This can help mitigate multicollinearity among correlated predictors and reduce the number of parameters that need to be estimated.

Before constructing the predictor matrix for PCA, we apply a $\log(1 + x)$ transformation to `departure_delay_in_minutes` and `arrival_delay_in_minutes`. As observed in the EDA, both variables are heavily right-skewed with a large mass of near-zero values and a long upper tail reaching over 1,500 minutes. Without correction, these extreme values can dominate the first principal components — causing them to capture delay variability rather than the structure genuinely relevant to passenger satisfaction. The $\log(1 + x)$ transformation (using `log1p()`) compresses the upper tail, reduces the influence of extreme outliers, and treats zero delays correctly since $\log(1 + 0) = 0$. The remaining predictors are left untransformed. Centering and scaling are handled internally by `prcomp()` via `center = TRUE, scale. = TRUE`.

We apply PCA to the **model matrix** (with dummy-encoded categoricals) to ensure all predictors are numeric. We apply PCA to the **model matrix** (with dummy-encoded categoricals) to ensure all predictors are numeric.

```{r pca-fit}
# Log1p-transform the two delay variables before PCA
# Applied to the imputed unscaled training data so that log1p acts on
# the original positive values (z-scored delays can be negative, so
# log1p must come before standardisation)
train_for_pca <- train %>%
  mutate(
    departure_delay_in_minutes = log1p(departure_delay_in_minutes),
    arrival_delay_in_minutes   = log1p(arrival_delay_in_minutes)
  )

# Build numeric model matrix (dummy-encode categoricals)
train_mm_pca <- as.data.frame(
  model.matrix(satisfaction ~ ., data = train_for_pca)[, -1]
)

# PCA on the predictor matrix
# center = TRUE and scale. = TRUE standardise all columns internally,
# so no prior manual scaling is needed here
pca_fit <- prcomp(train_mm_pca, center = TRUE, scale. = TRUE)

# Variance explained
pca_var <- summary(pca_fit)
pca_var
```

### Scree Plot and Cumulative Variance

```{r pca-scree, fig.height=5}
var_explained <- pca_var$importance[2, ]  # Proportion of variance
cum_var       <- pca_var$importance[3, ]  # Cumulative proportion

pca_df <- data.frame(
  PC = seq_along(var_explained),
  Variance = var_explained,
  Cumulative = cum_var
)

# Number of PCs for 90% variance
n_pc_90 <- min(which(cum_var >= 0.90))
cat("PCs needed for 90% variance:", n_pc_90, "\n")

p1 <- ggplot(pca_df, aes(x = PC, y = Variance)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#3498DB", alpha = 0.7, width = 0.7) +
  geom_line(color = "#2C3E50", linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_point(color = "#2C3E50", size = 2) +
  labs(title = "Scree Plot",
       x = "Principal Component", y = "Proportion of Variance") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 1:length(var_explained)) +
  theme_report(base_size = 11)

p2 <- ggplot(pca_df, aes(x = PC, y = Cumulative)) +
  geom_line(color = "#E74C3C", linewidth = 0.9) +
  geom_point(color = "#E74C3C", size = 2) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.90, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey40") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = n_pc_90, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey40") +
  annotate("text", x = n_pc_90 + 0.5, y = 0.85,
           label = paste0(n_pc_90, " PCs = 90%"), hjust = 0, size = 3.5,
           fontface = "bold", color = "grey30") +
  labs(title = "Cumulative Variance Explained",
       x = "Principal Component", y = "Cumulative Proportion") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 1:length(var_explained)) +
  theme_report(base_size = 11)

grid.arrange(p1, p2, ncol = 2)
```

The scree plot shows how much variance each principal component captures. The cumulative plot identifies how many components are needed to reach the 90% threshold. These plots provide context, but the actual number of components used in all subsequent PCA-based classifiers is determined by cross-validation AUC (see below), not the 90% threshold.

### PCR: Cross-Validation for Optimal Number of Components

Principal Components Regression (PCR) selects the number of components $M$ by cross-validation rather than by a fixed variance threshold. Following the ISLR v2 PCR approach (Section 6.3.1), we evaluate $M = 1, 2, \ldots, M_{\max}$ components using 10-fold cross-validation on the full training set. Instead of minimising MSE (as in the continuous response setting), we **maximise the cross-validation AUC**, since our outcome is binary.

**Pipeline approach:** In each fold, all preprocessing (imputation, log-transform, z-score and min-max scaling, dummy encoding) is fitted exclusively on the training portion of that fold and applied to the held-out portion. PCA is then computed on the scaled training matrix, and the learned rotation is applied to the held-out matrix — strictly no information from the held-out fold leaks into the transformation.

```{r pcr-cv, cache=TRUE}
# Max PCs to evaluate: n_pc_90 + 5 (or 30 max for computational speed)
max_pcs_try <- 23
pc_seq      <- seq_len(max_pcs_try)

set.seed(42)
n_folds_pcr  <- 10
pcr_fold_ids <- createFolds(train_with_na$satisfaction, k = n_folds_pcr, returnTrain = FALSE)

pcr_auc_mat <- matrix(NA_real_, nrow = max_pcs_try, ncol = n_folds_pcr)

cont_vars_pcr   <- c("age", "flight_distance",
                     "departure_delay_in_minutes", "arrival_delay_in_minutes")
rating_vars_pcr <- c("inflight_wifi_service", "departure_arrival_time_convenient",
                     "ease_of_online_booking", "gate_location", "food_and_drink",
                     "online_boarding", "seat_comfort", "inflight_entertainment",
                     "on_board_service", "leg_room_service", "baggage_handling",
                     "checkin_service", "inflight_service", "cleanliness")

for (f in seq_len(n_folds_pcr)) {
  cat(sprintf("\rPCR CV: fold %d / %d   ", f, n_folds_pcr))

  idx_val <- pcr_fold_ids[[f]]
  tr_raw  <- train_with_na[-idx_val, ]
  va_raw  <- train_with_na[idx_val, ]

  # 1. Impute arrival delay (fit model on training fold only)
  tr_comp <- tr_raw %>% filter(!is.na(arrival_delay_in_minutes))
  delay_m <- lm(arrival_delay_in_minutes ~ departure_delay_in_minutes, data = tr_comp)
  tr_raw$arrival_delay_in_minutes[is.na(tr_raw$arrival_delay_in_minutes)] <-
    predict(delay_m, newdata = tr_raw[is.na(tr_raw$arrival_delay_in_minutes), ])
  va_raw$arrival_delay_in_minutes[is.na(va_raw$arrival_delay_in_minutes)] <-
    predict(delay_m, newdata = va_raw[is.na(va_raw$arrival_delay_in_minutes), ])

  # 1b. Log1p-transform delay variables (consistent with pca-fit chunk above)
  tr_raw$departure_delay_in_minutes <- log1p(tr_raw$departure_delay_in_minutes)
  tr_raw$arrival_delay_in_minutes   <- log1p(tr_raw$arrival_delay_in_minutes)
  va_raw$departure_delay_in_minutes <- log1p(va_raw$departure_delay_in_minutes)
  va_raw$arrival_delay_in_minutes   <- log1p(va_raw$arrival_delay_in_minutes)

  # 2. Factor-encode response
  tr_y <- factor(tr_raw$satisfaction)
  va_y <- factor(va_raw$satisfaction)

  # 3. Build numeric model matrices (dummy-encode categoricals)
  tr_mm <- model.matrix(~ . - satisfaction,
                        data = tr_raw %>% mutate(satisfaction = factor(satisfaction)))[, -1]
  va_mm <- tryCatch(
    model.matrix(~ . - satisfaction,
                 data = va_raw %>% mutate(satisfaction = factor(satisfaction)))[, -1],
    error = function(e) NULL
  )
  if (is.null(va_mm)) next

  # Align columns (handle rare dummy levels absent from one fold)
  common_cols <- intersect(colnames(tr_mm), colnames(va_mm))
  tr_mm <- tr_mm[, common_cols, drop = FALSE]
  va_mm <- va_mm[, common_cols, drop = FALSE]

  # 4. Scale using training fold parameters (z-score for all columns)
  col_means <- colMeans(tr_mm)
  col_sds   <- apply(tr_mm, 2, sd)
  col_sds[col_sds == 0] <- 1
  tr_mm_s <- scale(tr_mm, center = col_means, scale = col_sds)
  va_mm_s <- scale(va_mm, center = col_means, scale = col_sds)

  # 5. PCA on training fold (data already centred/scaled → centre = FALSE)
  pca_f  <- prcomp(tr_mm_s, center = FALSE, scale. = FALSE)
  tr_pcs <- predict(pca_f, tr_mm_s)
  va_pcs <- predict(pca_f, va_mm_s)

  # 6. For each M, fit logistic regression on first M PCs and record AUC
  for (M in pc_seq) {
    if (M > ncol(tr_pcs)) break
    tr_df <- as.data.frame(tr_pcs[, 1:M, drop = FALSE])
    tr_df$satisfaction <- tr_y
    va_df <- as.data.frame(va_pcs[, 1:M, drop = FALSE])

    fit <- tryCatch(
      glm(satisfaction ~ ., data = tr_df, family = binomial),
      error = function(e) NULL
    )
    if (is.null(fit)) next

    probs <- predict(fit, va_df, type = "response")
    pcr_auc_mat[M, f] <- as.numeric(auc(
      roc(va_y, probs,
          levels = c("neutral or dissatisfied", "satisfied"),
          direction = "<", quiet = TRUE)
    ))
  }
}
cat("\nPCR cross-validation complete.\n")
```

```{r pcr-cv-results}
pcr_cv_df <- data.frame(
  M        = pc_seq,
  Mean_AUC = rowMeans(pcr_auc_mat, na.rm = TRUE),
  SD_AUC   = apply(pcr_auc_mat, 1, sd, na.rm = TRUE)
)

n_pc_max_auc <- pc_seq[which.max(pcr_cv_df$Mean_AUC)]

cat(sprintf("M with highest CV AUC: %d (AUC = %.4f)\n",
            n_pc_max_auc, max(pcr_cv_df$Mean_AUC, na.rm = TRUE)))
cat(sprintf("Number of PCs for 90%% variance: %d\n", n_pc_90))

# Use the CV-maximising M as the selected number of components
n_pc_optimal <- n_pc_max_auc
cat(sprintf("Selected M (CV-maximising): %d (AUC = %.4f)\n",
            n_pc_optimal, pcr_cv_df$Mean_AUC[n_pc_optimal]))
```

```{r pcr-cv-plot, fig.height=5, fig.width=10}
best_row <- pcr_cv_df[n_pc_optimal, ]

ggplot(pcr_cv_df, aes(x = M, y = Mean_AUC)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = Mean_AUC - SD_AUC, ymax = Mean_AUC + SD_AUC),
              alpha = 0.18, fill = "steelblue") +
  geom_line(color = "steelblue", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(color = "steelblue", size = 2) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = n_pc_optimal, linetype = "dashed",
             color = "tomato", linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = n_pc_90, linetype = "dotted",
             color = "grey50", linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_point(data = best_row, aes(x = M, y = Mean_AUC),
             color = "tomato", size = 4.5, shape = 21, stroke = 1.5, fill = "white") +
  annotate("text", x = n_pc_optimal + 0.4,
           y = best_row$Mean_AUC - 0.004,
           label = sprintf("Selected M = %d\nAUC = %.4f",
                           n_pc_optimal, best_row$Mean_AUC),
           hjust = 0, size = 3.5, color = "#C0392B", fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = n_pc_90 + 0.4,
           y = min(pcr_cv_df$Mean_AUC, na.rm = TRUE) + 0.002,
           label = paste0("90% var =\n", n_pc_90, " PCs"),
           hjust = 0, size = 3, color = "grey50") +
  labs(title = "PCR: 10-Fold Cross-Validated AUC vs. Number of Principal Components",
       subtitle = paste0("Red circle = selected M = ", n_pc_optimal,
                         " (highest CV AUC); dotted line = 90% variance threshold"),
       x = "Number of Principal Components (M)",
       y = "Mean 10-Fold CV AUC") +
  theme_report() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")
```

The CV AUC increases and reaches its maximum at $M = 22$, which we adopt as the optimal number of components. The difference in CV AUC between $M = 22$ and $M = 23$ is negligible (or effectively zero), indicating that nearly all predictive information is already captured. Since this is very close to the maximum possible number of components, the gain from dimensionality reduction is minimal, suggesting that PCA does not substantially improve predictive performance. Therefore, PCA-based models are expected to perform similarly to models using the full set of original predictors.

Note: We do not produce PCA-based decision boundary plots for LDA and QDA. Projecting the data onto just two principal components and then fitting a 2D classifier discards the information from all other components, which makes the resulting boundaries misleading. The LD1 histogram above already provides a more faithful representation of how LDA separates the classes using the full predictor space. For QDA, which does not produce a single discriminant axis, we focus instead on predictive performance metrics in the evaluation section below.

# Model Evaluation and Selection

Having explored the data and fitted individual models for interpretive purposes, we now turn to the central question of model selection. The test error is the relevant quantity for comparing models, and a proper evaluation requires that the test data play no role in the fitting process. We conduct a systematic comparison of five classification methods — Logistic Regression, LDA, QDA, KNN, and Naive Bayes — each fitted with **default hyperparameters** (no tuning) across three predictor configurations: the **Full** model (all predictors), the **BIC-reduced** model (19 predictors from forward stepwise selection), and the **PCA-transformed** data (using the 22 of principal components determined earlier). This yields a complete $5 \times 3 = 15$ model-data version grid.

To avoid data leakage, we perform all preprocessing within each evaluation step. All imputation models and scaling parameters are fitted exclusively on the training portion and applied to the validation/test portions. This ensures that no information from the evaluation data leaks into the preprocessing.

## Train–Validation–Test Split

We use the full `train.csv` (80% of the original data) as the training set. The `test.csv` (20% of the original data) is split 50/50 into a **validation set** (10% of total) for model selection and a **held-out test set** (10% of total) for final evaluation. This yields an 80–10–10 split overall. All imputation and scaling parameters are estimated from the training set only and applied to all sets.

```{r train-val-test-split}
set.seed(42)
test_idx <- sample(nrow(test_with_na), size = round(0.5 * nrow(test_with_na)))
val_raw  <- test_with_na[-test_idx, ]
test_holdout <- test_with_na[test_idx, ]

fit_raw <- train_with_na

cat("Training set:", nrow(fit_raw), "observations\n")
cat("Validation set:", nrow(val_raw), "observations\n")
cat("Held-out test set:", nrow(test_holdout), "observations\n")
cat("Class balance (train):", round(prop.table(table(fit_raw$satisfaction)) * 100, 1), "%\n")
cat("Class balance (val):", round(prop.table(table(val_raw$satisfaction)) * 100, 1), "%\n")
cat("Class balance (test):", round(prop.table(table(test_holdout$satisfaction)) * 100, 1), "%\n")
```

### Imputation and Scaling on the Training Set (No Leakage)

We fit the imputation model and compute all scaling parameters from the fitting set only, then apply them to both sets.

```{r leakage-free-preprocessing}
# --- 1. Imputation: fit on fitting set, apply to both ---
fit_reg <- fit_raw %>%
  dplyr::select(departure_delay_in_minutes, arrival_delay_in_minutes) %>%
  na.omit()

delay_model_fit <- lm(arrival_delay_in_minutes ~ departure_delay_in_minutes,
                      data = fit_reg)

train_fit <- fit_raw
train_val <- val_raw

train_fit$arrival_delay_in_minutes[is.na(train_fit$arrival_delay_in_minutes)] <-
  predict(delay_model_fit,
          newdata = train_fit[is.na(train_fit$arrival_delay_in_minutes), ])

train_val$arrival_delay_in_minutes[is.na(train_val$arrival_delay_in_minutes)] <-
  predict(delay_model_fit,
          newdata = train_val[is.na(train_val$arrival_delay_in_minutes), ])

cat("NAs remaining — fit:", sum(is.na(train_fit$arrival_delay_in_minutes)),
    " val:", sum(is.na(train_val$arrival_delay_in_minutes)), "\n")

# --- 1b. Log1p-transform delay variables (consistent with PCR CV pipeline) ---
# Applied after imputation (values are non-negative) and before z-score scaling
train_fit$departure_delay_in_minutes <- log1p(train_fit$departure_delay_in_minutes)
train_fit$arrival_delay_in_minutes   <- log1p(train_fit$arrival_delay_in_minutes)
train_val$departure_delay_in_minutes <- log1p(train_val$departure_delay_in_minutes)
train_val$arrival_delay_in_minutes   <- log1p(train_val$arrival_delay_in_minutes)

# --- 2. Convert target to factor ---
train_fit$satisfaction <- factor(train_fit$satisfaction)
train_val$satisfaction <- factor(train_val$satisfaction)

# --- 3. Scaling: compute parameters from fitting set only ---
cont_vars <- c("age", "flight_distance",
               "departure_delay_in_minutes", "arrival_delay_in_minutes")

rating_vars <- c("inflight_wifi_service", "departure_arrival_time_convenient",
                 "ease_of_online_booking", "gate_location", "food_and_drink",
                 "online_boarding", "seat_comfort", "inflight_entertainment",
                 "on_board_service", "leg_room_service", "baggage_handling",
                 "checkin_service", "inflight_service", "cleanliness")

# Standard scaling for continuous vars
fit_cont_means <- colMeans(train_fit[, cont_vars])
fit_cont_sds   <- apply(train_fit[, cont_vars], 2, sd)

train_fit[, cont_vars] <- scale(train_fit[, cont_vars],
                                center = fit_cont_means, scale = fit_cont_sds)
train_val[, cont_vars] <- scale(train_val[, cont_vars],
                                center = fit_cont_means, scale = fit_cont_sds)

# Min-max scaling for rating vars
fit_rating_mins   <- apply(train_fit[, rating_vars], 2, min)
fit_rating_ranges <- apply(train_fit[, rating_vars], 2, max) - fit_rating_mins

for (v in rating_vars) {
  train_fit[[v]] <- (train_fit[[v]] - fit_rating_mins[v]) / fit_rating_ranges[v]
  train_val[[v]] <- (train_val[[v]] - fit_rating_mins[v]) / fit_rating_ranges[v]
}

cat("Preprocessing complete.\n")
```

### Preparing the Three Predictor Sets

We construct three versions of the predictor matrix. For the PCA version, the principal component transformation is fitted on the **training (fitting) set only** and then applied to the validation set using `predict()`, so that the validation set contributes no information to the rotation — avoiding data leakage in the same way we handled imputation and scaling above.

```{r prepare-predictor-sets}
# --- BIC-reduced formula ---
bic_formula <- formula(step_fwd_bic)

# --- PCA predictor sets ---
# Fit PCA on the fitting set only; apply the learned rotation to the validation set
train_fit_mm <- as.data.frame(model.matrix(satisfaction ~ ., data = train_fit)[, -1])
train_val_mm <- as.data.frame(model.matrix(satisfaction ~ ., data = train_val)[, -1])

pca_fit_only <- prcomp(train_fit_mm, center = TRUE, scale. = TRUE)

# Use the CV-optimal M determined by PCR cross-validation above

train_fit_pca <- as.data.frame(predict(pca_fit_only, train_fit_mm)[, 1:n_pc_optimal])
train_val_pca <- as.data.frame(predict(pca_fit_only, train_val_mm)[, 1:n_pc_optimal])
train_fit_pca$satisfaction <- train_fit$satisfaction
train_val_pca$satisfaction <- train_val$satisfaction

cat("PCA components retained (CV-optimal by AUC):", n_pc_optimal, "\n")
```

```{r}
bic_formula
```

## Initial Model Comparison (Default Hyperparameters)

All models are fitted with **default hyperparameters** (no tuning). We report confusion matrices and classification metrics on the held-out validation set.

```{r fit-all-models-default, cache=TRUE}
library(class)   # KNN
library(e1071)   # Naive Bayes
# =============================================
# Fit all models and get predicted probabilities for "satisfied"
# =============================================

val_labels <- train_val$satisfaction

# --- 1. Logistic Regression (Full) ---
glm_full_fit <- glm(satisfaction ~ ., data = train_fit, family = binomial)
prob_glm_full <- predict(glm_full_fit, train_val, type = "response")

# --- 2. Logistic Regression (BIC reduced) ---
glm_bic_fit <- glm(bic_formula, data = train_fit, family = binomial)
prob_glm_bic <- predict(glm_bic_fit, train_val, type = "response")

# --- 3. Logistic Regression (PCA) ---
glm_pca_fit <- glm(satisfaction ~ ., data = train_fit_pca, family = binomial)
prob_glm_pca <- predict(glm_pca_fit, train_val_pca, type = "response")

# --- 4. LDA (Full) ---
lda_full_fit <- lda(satisfaction ~ ., data = train_fit)
prob_lda_full <- predict(lda_full_fit, train_val)$posterior[, "satisfied"]

# --- 5. LDA (BIC reduced) ---
lda_bic_fit <- lda(bic_formula, data = train_fit)
prob_lda_bic <- predict(lda_bic_fit, train_val)$posterior[, "satisfied"]

# --- 6. LDA (PCA) ---
lda_pca_fit <- lda(satisfaction ~ ., data = train_fit_pca)
prob_lda_pca <- predict(lda_pca_fit, train_val_pca)$posterior[, "satisfied"]

# --- 7. QDA (Full) ---
qda_full_fit <- qda(satisfaction ~ ., data = train_fit)
prob_qda_full <- predict(qda_full_fit, train_val)$posterior[, "satisfied"]

# --- 8. QDA (BIC reduced) ---
qda_bic_fit <- qda(bic_formula, data = train_fit)
prob_qda_bic <- predict(qda_bic_fit, train_val)$posterior[, "satisfied"]

# --- 9. QDA (PCA) ---
qda_pca_fit <- qda(satisfaction ~ ., data = train_fit_pca)
prob_qda_pca <- predict(qda_pca_fit, train_val_pca)$posterior[, "satisfied"]

# --- 10. KNN (Full, BIC, PCA) — default k=5 ---
train_fit_knn <- as.data.frame(model.matrix(satisfaction ~ ., data = train_fit)[, -1])
train_val_knn <- as.data.frame(model.matrix(satisfaction ~ ., data = train_val)[, -1])
train_fit_knn_bic <- as.data.frame(model.matrix(bic_formula, data = train_fit)[, -1])
train_val_knn_bic <- as.data.frame(model.matrix(bic_formula, data = train_val)[, -1])

knn_pred_full <- knn(train_fit_knn, train_val_knn,
                     cl = train_fit$satisfaction, k = 5, prob = TRUE)
knn_prob <- attr(knn_pred_full, "prob")
prob_knn_full <- ifelse(knn_pred_full == "satisfied", knn_prob, 1 - knn_prob)

knn_pred_bic <- knn(train_fit_knn_bic, train_val_knn_bic,
                    cl = train_fit$satisfaction, k = 5, prob = TRUE)
knn_prob_b <- attr(knn_pred_bic, "prob")
prob_knn_bic <- ifelse(knn_pred_bic == "satisfied", knn_prob_b, 1 - knn_prob_b)

knn_pred_pca <- knn(train_fit_pca[, -ncol(train_fit_pca)],
                    train_val_pca[, -ncol(train_val_pca)],
                    cl = train_fit$satisfaction, k = 5, prob = TRUE)
knn_prob_p <- attr(knn_pred_pca, "prob")
prob_knn_pca <- ifelse(knn_pred_pca == "satisfied", knn_prob_p, 1 - knn_prob_p)

# --- 11. Naive Bayes (Full, BIC, PCA) ---
nb_full_fit <- naiveBayes(satisfaction ~ ., data = train_fit)
prob_nb_full <- predict(nb_full_fit, train_val, type = "raw")[, "satisfied"]

nb_bic_fit <- naiveBayes(bic_formula, data = train_fit)
prob_nb_bic <- predict(nb_bic_fit, train_val, type = "raw")[, "satisfied"]

nb_pca_fit <- naiveBayes(satisfaction ~ ., data = train_fit_pca)
prob_nb_pca <- predict(nb_pca_fit, train_val_pca, type = "raw")[, "satisfied"]

cat("All models fitted successfully.\n")
```

### Validation Set Metrics

```{r initial-metrics-table}
# Collect all probabilities
all_probs <- list(
  "GLM Full"  = prob_glm_full,  "GLM BIC"  = prob_glm_bic,  "GLM PCA"  = prob_glm_pca,
  "LDA Full"  = prob_lda_full,  "LDA BIC"  = prob_lda_bic,  "LDA PCA"  = prob_lda_pca,
  "QDA Full"  = prob_qda_full,  "QDA BIC"  = prob_qda_bic,  "QDA PCA"  = prob_qda_pca,
  "KNN5 Full" = prob_knn_full,  "KNN5 BIC" = prob_knn_bic,  "KNN5 PCA" = prob_knn_pca,
  "NB Full"   = prob_nb_full,   "NB BIC"   = prob_nb_bic,   "NB PCA"   = prob_nb_pca
)

# Helper: compute metrics
compute_metrics <- function(probs, labels, threshold = 0.5) {
  pred_class <- factor(ifelse(probs >= threshold, "satisfied", "neutral or dissatisfied"),
                       levels = levels(labels))
  cm <- confusionMatrix(pred_class, labels, positive = "satisfied")
  roc_obj <- roc(labels, probs,
                 levels = c("neutral or dissatisfied", "satisfied"),
                 direction = "<", quiet = TRUE)
  prec <- cm$byClass["Precision"]
  rec  <- cm$byClass["Recall"]
  f1   <- ifelse(prec + rec > 0, 2 * prec * rec / (prec + rec), 0)
  data.frame(
    Accuracy  = round(cm$overall["Accuracy"], 4),
    Precision = round(prec, 4),
    Recall    = round(rec, 4),
    F1        = round(f1, 4),
    AUC       = round(as.numeric(auc(roc_obj)), 4)
  )
}

# Build metrics table
metrics_list <- lapply(names(all_probs), function(nm) {
  m <- compute_metrics(all_probs[[nm]], val_labels)
  m$Model <- nm
  m
})
metrics_df <- bind_rows(metrics_list) %>%
  dplyr::select(Model, Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1, AUC) %>%
  arrange(desc(AUC))

metrics_df %>%
  kable(row.names = FALSE,
        caption = "Initial Model Comparison — Validation Set Metrics (Default Hyperparameters)") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE)
```

### Selecting the Best Model–Data Version Combinations

We use **AUC (Area Under the ROC Curve)** as the primary metric for model selection. This choice is motivated by two considerations. First, the dataset exhibits a moderate class imbalance (\~57% dissatisfied vs \~43% satisfied), which means that raw accuracy can be misleading — a model that simply predicts the majority class would achieve \~57% accuracy without learning anything useful. AUC, by contrast, evaluates the model's ability to discriminate between the two classes across *all possible classification thresholds*, making it robust to class imbalance. Second, AUC directly measures the probability that a randomly chosen satisfied passenger receives a higher predicted probability than a randomly chosen dissatisfied passenger — a threshold-free summary of overall separability that is particularly informative when the optimal decision threshold is not known in advance.

Rather than simply picking the top 4 individual configurations, we identify the **best 2 models** and the **best 2 data versions** and form their 4 combinations. This structured approach lets us disentangle the effect of the model choice from the effect of the predictor set, and ensures we carry forward configurations that are strong on both dimensions.

```{r select-best-combinations}
# Parse model name into method + data version
metrics_df <- metrics_df %>%
  mutate(
    Method      = sub(" .*", "", Model),
    DataVersion = sub(".* ", "", Model)
  )

# --- Best 2 models: average AUC across all 3 data versions ---
model_avg <- metrics_df %>%
  group_by(Method) %>%
  summarise(Mean_AUC = round(mean(AUC), 4), .groups = "drop") %>%
  arrange(desc(Mean_AUC))

cat("=== Average AUC by Model (across all data versions) ===\n")
model_avg %>%
  kable(row.names = FALSE, col.names = c("Model", "Mean AUC")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = "striped", full_width = FALSE)

best_2_models <- model_avg$Method[1:2]
cat("\nBest 2 models:", paste(best_2_models, collapse = ", "), "\n")

# --- Best 2 data versions: average AUC across all models ---
dv_avg <- metrics_df %>%
  group_by(DataVersion) %>%
  summarise(Mean_AUC = round(mean(AUC), 4), .groups = "drop") %>%
  arrange(desc(Mean_AUC))

cat("\n=== Average AUC by Data Version (across all models) ===\n")
dv_avg %>%
  kable(row.names = FALSE, col.names = c("Data Version", "Mean AUC")) %>%
```
